questions.question: Who is the author of 'Three Days to See'?

options.B: Anne Sullivan

questions.explanation: Helen Keller, who was both blind and deaf, wrote this essay about her appreciation for sight.

questions.question: What is the main theme of the story?

options.A: How to become a famous writer
options.B: Appreciating the gift of sight and the senses
options.C: How to survive in a forest
options.D: The history of the blind school

questions.explanation: The story encourages people to use their senses to the fullest.

questions.question: If Helen Keller were given sight for three days, whom would she want to see first?

options.B: Her teacher, Mrs. Anne Sullivan Macy

questions.explanation: She wanted to see the person who opened the world of language and education to her.

questions.question: On which day did Helen Keller want to see the dawn and the history of the world?

options.B: The second day

questions.explanation: The second day was devoted to seeing the history of the world through museums and nature.

questions.question: What does Helen Keller find surprising about people with sight?

options.B: They often see very little
options.D: They can see in the dark

questions.explanation: She observed that those with sight often take it for granted and don't notice the beauty around them.

questions.question: What would Helen Keller do on her third day?

options.B: Go to the city to see people's daily lives

questions.explanation: She wanted to spend the final day observing the work and daily lives of ordinary people.

questions.question: Helen Keller lost her sight and hearing at what age?

options.B: Ten years old

questions.explanation: A childhood illness took away her sight and hearing when she was a toddler.

questions.question: What part of the tree does Helen Keller enjoy feeling most?

options.B: The smooth skin of a silver birch

questions.explanation: She often describes the tactile beauty of the silver birch and the rough bark of a pine.

questions.question: What is the 'motto' Helen Keller suggests for everyone?

options.B: Use your eyes as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind

questions.explanation: She believes people would appreciate their senses more if they thought they might lose them.

questions.question: How does Helen Keller 'see' things currently?

options.B: Through her fingertips

questions.explanation: She uses her sense of touch to understand the world around her.

questions.question: What would she look for in the eyes of her friends?

options.B: The 'inner character' that reveals their soul

questions.explanation: She wanted to see the outward manifestation of the kindness she felt through touch.

questions.question: Where would she spend a large part of her second day?

options.B: In museums of art and natural history

questions.explanation: Museums would show her the history of human progress and nature.

questions.question: Why did Helen Keller want to see a theater or a movie?

options.B: To see the grace of human rhythmic movement

questions.explanation: She was fascinated by the idea of visual art and performance.

questions.question: On the third day, she would go to New York to see the _____.

options.B: Quiet parks

questions.explanation: She wanted to see the hustle and bustle of modern life.

questions.question: What did she ask her friend who had just returned from a walk in the woods?

options.B: What did you observe?

questions.explanation: She was disappointed when her friend said she saw 'nothing in particular'.

questions.question: According to Helen Keller, darkness makes sight more _____.

options.B: Precious

questions.explanation: The loss or lack of something makes its presence much more valuable.

questions.question: What would she want to see in her home on the first day?

options.B: The small things that make a house a home

questions.explanation: She valued the familiar objects that had been her companions in the dark.

questions.question: What sensation does she feel when she puts her hand on a small tree?

options.B: The happy quiver of a bird in full song

questions.explanation: Her sense of touch is so sensitive she can feel the vibrations of a bird singing.

questions.question: What kind of faces would she want to see in the city?

options.B: Faces of people showing all kinds of emotions

questions.explanation: She wanted to see the reality of human experience, including both joy and sorrow.

questions.question: Helen Keller says she can feel the 'magnificent symmetry' of what?

options.B: A leaf

questions.explanation: She finds great beauty in the structure of leaves and flowers through touch.

questions.question: What does the word 'panoramic' describe in the story?

options.B: A wide, complete view of something

questions.explanation: She wants a panoramic view of the world’s history and progress.

questions.question: The story 'Three Days to See' is an invitation to be more _____.

options.B: Rich

questions.explanation: The essay urges us to pay attention to the world around us.

questions.question: How does Helen Keller feel about her three days of imagined sight?

options.B: She knows she hasn't seen half of what she wanted
options.C: She thinks it would be boring
options.D: She doesn't want to see at all

questions.explanation: Three days is too short a time to see the vast beauty of the world.

questions.question: What is the primary sense Helen Keller uses to 'read' people's faces now?

options.B: Touch

questions.explanation: She 'sees' a face by touching it with her fingers.

questions.question: The essay concludes by reminding us to make the most of _____.

options.B: Every sense we possess

questions.explanation: She encourages the full use of sight, sound, smell, and touch as a way to truly live.
